utterances: If countries - either historically because of old nuclear programs, or currently in terms of how they operate their nuclear energy facilities - are leaving a bunch of material out there that could potentially fall in the hands of terrorists, that poses an extraordinary threat to the United States, to South Korea and to countries all around the world.
The president also said he believes the U.S.
can make deeper cuts in its nuclear arsenal and still provide protection for itself and its allies.
He did not say how deep the cuts could go.
Mike Shuster, NPR News, Seoul.

A coroner in Britain has ruled that some British troops who died in Afghanistan were, in his words, unlawfully killed, and the blame is being directed not at the Taliban but rather at Britain's defense establishment.
Vicki Barker has more from London.
Corporal Sarah Bryant and Special Forces Reservists Sean Reeve, Richard Larkin and Paul Stout were killed when their Land Rover set off a massive roadside bomb in Helmand Province in 2008.
Under British law, inquests are held on all U.K.
citizens who die abroad to establish the cause of death.

And in a highly unusual finding for troops who fell in combat, the coroner in this case ruled that the four were unlawfully killed - by implication not so much by enemies in the field as by inadequacies in their training and their equipment.
(Soundbite of news broadcast)
Today at five, unlawful killing.
The inquest verdict of the deaths of four British soldiers in Afghanistan.
The news swiftly became the top story here.
Prime Minister Gordon Brown's political rivals were quick to condemn the Labour government's handling of British operations in Afghanistan.
Nick Harvey is a lawmaker with Britain's third party, the Liberal Democrats.
Well, this is a scathing verdict from the coroner, and it's a damning indictment to the preparations the Ministry of Defense made when sending our people into really very dangerous situations.

The news was particularly damaging to Brown because it came just days after he told the Chilcot inquiry into the Iraq War that both as finance minister and then as prime minister he's given the military everything they've asked for.
Liam Fox will be defense minister if his opposition Conservative Party wins the next election.
He says Brown should be grilled again.
This is in direct contradiction to what the prime minister said about equipment levels and shows why he needs to be recalled to Chilcot.
Thirty-seven British troops have died in the lightly armored Snatch vehicles in Iraq and Afghanistan.
Colonel Richard Camp is a former commander of Britain's Afghan mission.
He says years of underspending meant the army had to go to war with what they had to hand - armored vehicles left over from northern Ireland.
These Snatches were designed for riot control at best, to deal with blast bombs and bricks being hurled at them, rather than the kind of explosives they're facing in Afghanistan.
Clearly our training regime at that stage could have been better.
We want to and we will respond to both the coroner and the families.
It's in front of a big crowd, yeah.

And then they get up and they go backstage, and that's when the action happens.
So yeah, there is a big crowd, but I think unless you're backstage and you see what's going on, you would think, huh, I could do that.
There's no big deal there.

But then there's another woman you wrote about, Catherine Burns.

Yes.

I spoke to her much later.
She said the cramping is quite intense.
And at a certain point, she said I'm sitting and my body just told me that's not good enough and I had to kind of lie down and double up in pain.
So she was affected not quite - she wasn't taken away in an ambulance as was the MP from Myanmar, and a couple of other people were taken away.

Hi, Derrick, you're on TALK OF THE NATION.
Hi.
Hi.
Thanks for taking my call.
Sure.
Well, a couple of years back, my friends purchased a vial of the world's hottest - supposedly the world's hottest hot sauce ghost pepper sauce.
And as a prank to my other friend, he switched it out with a milder hot sauce and switched the labels around.
And it was all fun and games until we almost thought we should be calling an ambulance for our other friend who was collapsed on his knees crying in pain.
Wow.
And that's obviously not psychosomatic He did not know that he had ingested this really hot sauce.
No. He had no idea.
It was - he took a full scoop of a scooped chip and filled it to the brim.
Oh, man.
But then...
I want to say - let me just say, the ghost pepper, that is the Naga - it's another name for the one that we were just talking about.
So that is...
(Unintelligible)
Yeah, that's the Naga King chili.
But, you know, we think we just learned our lesson at that one.
But another friend of ours who is about 300 - 350-pound guy who plays football, he filled up his chip.
He eats the thing and hardly reacts to it at all.
He just, that's too hot.
(LAUGHTER)
And then he fills up another one, continues to eat the chip.
He missed out on the drama with the first person, but yeah.
All right.
Derrick, thanks for your call.
But you raised for us, a really good point.
And I want to put to Mary 'cause I know you researched kind of the science behind this.
Why do people have such dramatically different reactions?
Is it life experience or genetics or what?
It's one of two things and possibly both.
You eat a lot of hot peppers, a lot of capsaicin, you burn out the pain receptors that cause the pain - that cause you to feel the pain.
So you build up a tolerance that way.
So I don't know if the 350-pound guy eats a lot of hot peppers.
That may be why.
But there's also genetic differences.
Some people have more of these pain receptors than others.
So damage is actually done.
Tissue damage is done by the chemicals.

Actually, it - no, not - usually, what's going on the mouth is - the way that capsaicin works - there are these pain receptors that respond to a certain temperature, but the capsaicin triggers them without the heat.
So it fools you into - it tricks a human being into going, oh, this is hot.
I got to get it out of my mouth or I'm going to burn myself.
In fact, it's not hot enough to burn the mouth that way.
It's a way to get you to spit it out.
And it's a survival mechanism.
It's a brain trick then, really.
It's a brain trick, yeah.
But it can burn out - you used the term that it can burn out the taste receptors.
So at a certain point...
Yeah, it burns them out, and yet it's not the same way as holding a match to them.
But it damages them, yeah.
I did a story for ""Nightline"" a few years ago where I went to Avery Islands in Louisiana where Tabasco sauce is made.
And they mash up their peppers and then they pour in salt and then they let them sit in barrels for three years.
And then they open the mash and they water it down.
And it's had some time to ferment and get spicy.

So they had this little ceremony planned for me where I had to touch a little - my pinkie into a tiny, tiny drop and put it on my tongue and then I had to immediately follow it with a shot of bourbon.
And honestly, you know, this is all for their entertainment, you know, to get the Yankee to fall to the ground, and I almost fell to the ground.
It was really, really hot and it persisted for several hours.
And I'm just a little worried that on the scale that you were talking about, the Tabasco is really, really at the mild wimpy end compared to the Naga King chili.
Am I right?
Well, it's - yeah, it's a D.

But, you know, what is on to me is that I tried a little sliver of the Naga King chili because I wanted - well, first, because I'm a wimp but second, because I wanted to be able to actually taste the flavor.
And it has an exquisite kind of lemony, grassy, floral flavor.
It's like no chili pepper I've ever had.
